news_summary: NASA advances space exploration with ESCAPADE mission to study space weather between Earth and Mars, while JPL achieves progress in 3D-printed spacecraft components.
news_summary_fr: La NASA fait progresser l'exploration spatiale avec la mission ESCAPADE visant à étudier la météorologie spatiale entre la Terre et Mars, tandis que le JPL réalise des avancées dans le domaine des composants de vaisseaux spatiaux imprimés en 3D.
news_summary_es: La NASA avanza en la exploración espacial con la misión ESCAPADE para estudiar el clima espacial entre la Tierra y Marte, mientras que el JPL logra avances en los componentes de naves espaciales impresos en 3D.

articles.title: NASA’s ESCAPADE Ready to Study Space Weather from Earth to Mars
articles.summary: Mars is not what it used to be. Once warm, watery, and blanketed by a thick atmosphere, today the Red Planet is cold, dry, and draped by a thin atmospheric veil. The main culprit is a relentless stream of particles from the Sun, known as the solar wind. Over billions of years, the solar wind has stripped away […]
articles.content:
Mars is not what it used to be. Once warm, watery, and blanketed by a thick atmosphere, today the Red Planet is cold, dry, and draped by a thin atmospheric veil.

The main culprit is a relentless stream of particles from the Sun, known as the solar wind. Over billions of years, the solar wind has stripped away much of the Martian atmosphere, causing the planet to cool and its surface water to evaporate.

Now, NASA’s ESCAPADE (Escape and Plasma Acceleration and Dynamics Explorers) mission, which launched on Nov. 13, 2025, has turned on the science instruments that will investigate how this happened and how the Sun continues to influence the Red Planet. The science instruments, which are all operating as of Feb. 25, also will study space weather in new ways near Earth and on the way to Mars.

At Mars, ESCAPADE’s findings could also help NASA protect future explorers from the harsh Martian conditions.

“The pioneering ESCAPADE duo will not only investigate the Sun’s role in transforming Mars into an uninhabitable planet, but also will help inform the development of space weather protocols for solar events directed at Mars during future human missions to the Red Planet,” said Joe Westlake, heliophysics division director at NASA Headquarters in Washington. “By joining the heliophysics fleet of missions across the solar system, ESCAPADE will be another weather station making humans and technology in space safer and more successful.”

NASA’s ESCAPADE (Escape and Plasma Acceleration and Dynamics Explorers) mission launched on Nov. 13, 2025, atop a Blue Origin New Glenn rocket at Launch Complex 36 at Cape Canaveral Space Force Station in Florida. Blue Origin

First of its kind

With its twin spacecraft, ESCAPADE is the first science mission to coordinate two orbiters around Mars, gaining a perspective we’ve never had before. Together, the ESCAPADE twins will measure short-term changes in the magnetized environment around Mars, called the magnetosphere, and uncover real-time processes driving the planet’s atmospheric escape.

“Having two spacecraft is going to help us understand cause and effect — how the solar wind, when it comes to Mars, interacts with the magnetic field,” said Michele Cash, ESCAPADE program scientist at NASA Headquarters.

The ESCAPADE orbiters build on earlier Mars missions that have studied Mars' atmosphere, but with just one spacecraft.

“The ESCAPADE mission is a game changer,” said Rob Lillis, the mission’s principal investigator at the University of California, Berkeley. “It gives us what you might call a stereo perspective — two different vantage points simultaneously.”

Once ESCAPADE reaches Mars, its twin spacecraft will follow each other in the same orbit, passing over the same areas at different times to uncover when and where changes are happening.

“When we have two spacecraft crossing those regions in quick succession, we can monitor how those regions vary on timescales as short as two minutes,” Lillis said. “This will allow us to make measurements we could never make before.”

After six months, the two spacecraft will shift into different orbits, with one traveling farther from Mars and the other staying closer to it. Planned to last for five months, this second formation aims to study the solar wind and Martian magnetosphere simultaneously, allowing scientists to investigate how Mars responds to the solar wind in real time.

“Prior spacecraft could either be in the upstream solar wind, or they could be close to the planet measuring its magnetosphere,” Lillis said, “but ESCAPADE allows us to be in two places at once and to simultaneously measure the cause and the effect.”

Preparing for human exploration

When people set foot on Mars, they will not be as well protected from solar radiation as their family and friends on Earth.

Earth can withstand the solar wind’s ceaseless onslaught because it has a hardy magnetic field that shields us from the Sun’s energetic particles. However, Mars’ once robust magnetic field has weakened over time. Today it’s a patchwork of localized magnetism in the planet’s crust along with an ever-changing magnetic field generated by the solar wind’s interaction with charged particles in Mars’ upper atmosphere.

Mars has a hybrid magnetosphere made up of an induced magnetic field from the solar wind and crustal magnetic fields from the planet’s surface. In this artist’s concept yellow lines represent magnetic field lines from the Sun carried by the solar wind and blue lines represent Martian surface magnetic fields. White sparks indicate reconnection activity, where field lines break and reconnect, and red lines are reconnected magnetic fields that link the Martian surface to space.

Anil Rao/Univ. of Colorado/MAVEN/NASA GSFC

This “hybrid” magnetosphere provides little protection against the atmosphere-stripping force of the solar wind. This, plus Mars’ thin atmosphere, allows the Sun’s energetic particles to easily reach the Martian surface, endangering future human explorers there.

“Before we send humans to Mars, we need to understand what type of environment these astronauts are going to encounter,” Cash said.

Additionally, ESCAPADE will provide more information about Mars’ ionosphere — part of the upper atmosphere that future astronauts will use to send radio and navigation signals around the planet, as we do on Earth.

“If we ever want GPS at Mars or long-distance communications, we need to understand the ionosphere,” Lillis said.

Unique journey to Mars

Previous Mars missions have launched when Earth and Mars are aligned in their orbits, which only happens every 26 months. But ESCAPADE launched early, pioneering a new strategy that allows Mars-bound spacecraft to launch almost anytime.

Instead of heading directly to Mars, ESCAPADE’s spacecraft are first looping around a location in space a million miles from Earth called Lagrange point 2. In November 2026, when Earth and Mars are aligned, the ESCAPADE spacecraft will return to Earth and use our planet’s gravity to slingshot themselves toward Mars for a September 2027 arrival.

NASA’s two ESCAPADE spacecraft are not traveling directly from Earth to Mars but are first making a kidney-bean-shaped loop around a location in space called Lagrange point 2 (L2). A small black triangle shows approximately where the spacecraft were on Feb. 24, 2026. In November 2026, when Earth and Mars are more closely aligned in their orbits, the spacecraft will return to Earth and use our planet’s gravity to slingshot their way to Mars. Advanced Space

This unique “loiter” orbit will extend approximately 2 million miles from our planet, making the ESCAPADE spacecraft the first to fly through a previously unexplored region of Earth’s distant magnetotail, part of Earth’s magnetosphere opposite the Sun.

“We’re going to be doing some discovery science,” Lillis said. “No one has ever measured Earth’s tail this far away.”

The solar wind compresses the Sunward side of Earth’s magnetosphere and stretches the opposite side into a long tail, called the magnetotail. The two ESCAPADE spacecraft (indicated here in cyan) will be the first to fly through the distant part of Earth’s magnetotail, about 1.2 million miles from Earth, before heading to Mars. NASA Scientific Visualization Studio

Later, during their 10-month cruise to Mars, ESCAPADE’s two spacecraft will study solar wind and the interplanetary magnetic environment that Mars-bound astronauts will also traverse, preparing for future journeys to the Red Planet.

The ESCAPADE mission is funded by NASA’s Heliophysics Division and is part of the NASA Small Innovative Missions for Planetary Exploration program. UC Berkeley’s Space Sciences Laboratory leads the mission with key partners Rocket Lab; NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, Maryland; Embry-Riddle Aeronautical University; Advanced Space; and Blue Origin.

by Vanessa Thomas

NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center, Greenbelt, Md.
articles.reasoning: The article describes NASA's ESCAPADE mission, which is a pioneering dual-spacecraft mission studying Mars' atmospheric loss and space weather from Earth to Mars. This mission has broad scientific significance for understanding planetary atmospheres, solar wind effects, and preparing for future human exploration of Mars by improving safety protocols. It provides substantial context and details about the mission's objectives, innovative approach, and potential benefits for space science and human spaceflight.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s ESCAPADE mission begins studying Mars and space weather with twin spacecraft
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Mars used to be warm and full of water with a thick atmosphere, but today it is cold and dry. Scientists believe the solar wind, a steady stream of particles from the Sun, slowly stripped away much of Mars’ atmosphere over billions of years.

**What happened** – NASA launched the ESCAPADE mission on November 13, 2025. It includes two spacecraft working together to study how the Sun’s solar wind affects Mars and space weather between Earth and Mars. Both spacecraft have started their science work as of February 25, 2026.

**Impact** – ESCAPADE is the first mission to use two orbiters around Mars at the same time. This allows scientists to see how the solar wind interacts with Mars’ magnetic environment from two places. It helps us understand why Mars lost its atmosphere and how solar wind changes the planet’s space environment in real time. This knowledge is important to protect future astronauts from harmful solar radiation on Mars.

**What’s next step** – After six months, the spacecraft will change their orbits to study Mars’ magnetosphere and solar wind from different distances. In November 2026, they will return near Earth to use its gravity to speed up toward Mars, arriving in September 2027. During their trip, they will explore parts of Earth’s magnetotail never studied before and gather data about space weather on the way to Mars.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s ESCAPADE mission uses two spacecraft to study how the Sun’s solar wind shapes Mars’ atmosphere and space weather, helping prepare for safer human missions to the Red Planet.
articles.personality_title_fr: La mission ESCAPADE de la NASA commence à étudier Mars et la météo spatiale avec deux engins spatiaux
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Mars était autrefois chaude, pleine d’eau et couverte d’une atmosphère épaisse, mais aujourd’hui elle est froide et sèche. Les scientifiques pensent que le vent solaire, un flux constant de particules venant du Soleil, a lentement arraché une grande partie de l’atmosphère de Mars pendant des milliards d’années.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La NASA a lancé la mission ESCAPADE le 13 novembre 2025. Elle comprend deux engins spatiaux qui travaillent ensemble pour étudier comment le vent solaire affecte Mars et la météo spatiale entre la Terre et Mars. Les deux engins ont commencé leur travail scientifique le 25 février 2026.

**Impact** – ESCAPADE est la première mission à utiliser deux orbiteurs autour de Mars en même temps. Cela permet aux scientifiques de voir comment le vent solaire interagit avec l’environnement magnétique de Mars depuis deux endroits différents. Cela aide à comprendre pourquoi Mars a perdu son atmosphère et comment le vent solaire modifie l’environnement spatial de la planète en temps réel. Ces connaissances sont importantes pour protéger les futurs astronautes des radiations solaires dangereuses sur Mars.

**Prochaine étape** – Après six mois, les engins changeront d’orbite pour étudier la magnétosphère de Mars et le vent solaire à différentes distances. En novembre 2026, ils reviendront près de la Terre pour utiliser sa gravité et se propulser vers Mars, où ils arriveront en septembre 2027. Pendant le voyage, ils exploreront une partie de la magnétotail de la Terre jamais étudiée auparavant et collecteront des données sur la météo spatiale en route vers Mars.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La mission ESCAPADE de la NASA utilise deux engins spatiaux pour étudier comment le vent solaire façonne l’atmosphère de Mars et la météo spatiale, aidant à préparer des missions humaines plus sûres vers la planète rouge.
articles.personality_title_es: La misión ESCAPADE de la NASA comienza a estudiar Marte y el clima espacial con dos naves espaciales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Marte solía ser cálido, con mucha agua y una atmósfera gruesa, pero hoy es frío y seco. Los científicos creen que el viento solar, un flujo constante de partículas del Sol, quitó lentamente gran parte de la atmósfera de Marte durante miles de millones de años.

**Qué pasó** – La NASA lanzó la misión ESCAPADE el 13 de noviembre de 2025. Incluye dos naves espaciales que trabajan juntas para estudiar cómo el viento solar afecta a Marte y el clima espacial entre la Tierra y Marte. Ambas naves comenzaron su trabajo científico el 25 de febrero de 2026.

**Impacto** – ESCAPADE es la primera misión que usa dos orbitadores alrededor de Marte al mismo tiempo. Esto permite a los científicos ver cómo el viento solar interactúa con el ambiente magnético de Marte desde dos lugares diferentes. Ayuda a entender por qué Marte perdió su atmósfera y cómo el viento solar cambia el espacio alrededor del planeta en tiempo real. Esta información es importante para proteger a futuros astronautas de la radiación solar dañina en Marte.

**Próximo paso** – Después de seis meses, las naves cambiarán sus órbitas para estudiar la magnetosfera de Marte y el viento solar desde diferentes distancias. En noviembre de 2026, regresarán cerca de la Tierra para usar su gravedad y acelerar hacia Marte, llegando en septiembre de 2027. Durante el viaje, explorarán una parte nunca antes estudiada de la cola magnética de la Tierra y recopilarán datos sobre el clima espacial en el camino a Marte.

**Resumen en una frase** – La misión ESCAPADE de la NASA usa dos naves espaciales para estudiar cómo el viento solar afecta la atmósfera de Marte y el clima espacial, ayudando a preparar misiones humanas más seguras al Planeta Rojo.
articles.image_prompt: Two elegant, stylized spacecraft orbiting a warm-toned, softly glowing Mars with delicate magnetic field lines weaving between the planet and a radiant sun in the background, symbolizing the solar wind’s interaction and the pioneering dual-orbiter mission exploring space weather from Earth to Mars.

articles.title: JPL 3D-Printed Part Springs Forward
articles.summary: Description With a simple motion, a jack-in-the-box-like spring designed at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory showed the potential of additive manufacturing, also known as 3D printing, to cut costs and complexity for futuristic space antennas. Called JPL Additive Compliant Canister (JACC), the spring deployed on the small commercial spacecraft Proteus Space’s Mercury One on Feb. 3, 2026. […]
articles.content:
Description

With a simple motion, a jack-in-the-box-like spring designed at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory showed the potential of additive manufacturing, also known as 3D printing, to cut costs and complexity for futuristic space antennas. Called JPL Additive Compliant Canister (JACC), the spring deployed on the small commercial spacecraft Proteus Space’s Mercury One on Feb. 3, 2026. An onboard camera captured this video of the spring popping out of its container as the spacecraft passed over the Pacific Ocean in low Earth orbit.

Figure A

Figure A is a still image of JACC after deployment, taken above Antarctica.

JACC is one of two JPL payloads on the spacecraft that are demonstrating new technologies designed to take up reduced volume while precisely deploying antennas on future orbiters. JACC’s success demonstrates that 3D-printed mechanisms can be built faster, cheaper, and with less complexity than traditionally fabricated space hardware.

Printed out of titanium, JACC uses three times fewer parts than similar structures: Combined into a single part is a hinge, panel, compression spring, and two torsion springs. Weighing just over 1 pound (498 grams), it is about 4 inches (10 centimeters) on each side. The spring, which extends from a packed height of just over 1 inch to about 6 inches (3 centimeters to 15 centimeters), is modeled after communication antennas commonly used on satellites.

The second demonstration payload aboard Mercury One is the Solid Underconstrained Multi-Frequency (SUM) Deployable Antenna for Earth Science. Together with JACC, the two payloads go by the name Prototype Actuated Nonlinear Deployables Offering Repeatable Accuracy Stowed on a Box (PANDORASBox). They were both conceived, built, tested, and delivered for flight by JPL in less than one year on minimal budgets.

Mercury One launched from Vandenberg Space Force Base in California on Nov. 28, 2025, as part of SpaceX’s Transporter-15 mission.

JPL internal research development funds supported JACC, as did NASA’s Earth Science Technology Office (ESTO).
articles.reasoning: The article describes a successful demonstration of a 3D-printed spring mechanism by NASA's JPL that can reduce cost, complexity, and weight for future space antennas. This technological breakthrough has broad implications for space exploration and satellite technology, benefiting society by enabling more efficient and affordable space missions. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a tangible advancement with significant potential impact.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s 3D-Printed Spring Successfully Unfolds in Space
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) is exploring new ways to build parts for spacecraft using 3D printing. This method, called additive manufacturing, can create complex parts faster and cheaper than traditional methods.

**What happened** – On February 3, 2026, a small spacecraft named Mercury One, launched from California in November 2025, tested a special spring called the JPL Additive Compliant Canister (JACC). This spring, made from titanium and 3D-printed as a single piece, popped out like a jack-in-the-box while orbiting Earth. A camera on the spacecraft recorded the spring’s smooth deployment over the Pacific Ocean.

**Impact** – The JACC spring is unique because it combines several parts into one, making it lighter, simpler, and less expensive to produce. It uses three times fewer parts than similar traditional springs and is about the size of a small box. This successful test shows that 3D printing can help build better space antennas that fold small for launch and open precisely in space.

**What's next step** – JPL will continue to develop and test these 3D-printed parts along with other new antenna designs on future missions. These technologies could help make satellites and space instruments smaller, cheaper, and more reliable.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s JPL proved that 3D-printed parts can simplify and improve space hardware by successfully deploying a lightweight spring in orbit.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le ressort imprimé en 3D de la NASA se déploie avec succès dans l'espace
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Laboratoire de propulsion à réaction (JPL) de la NASA explore de nouvelles façons de fabriquer des pièces pour les engins spatiaux grâce à l'impression 3D, aussi appelée fabrication additive. Cette méthode permet de créer des pièces complexes plus rapidement et à moindre coût que les méthodes traditionnelles.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le 3 février 2026, un petit engin spatial nommé Mercury One, lancé en Californie en novembre 2025, a testé un ressort spécial appelé JPL Additive Compliant Canister (JACC). Ce ressort, fabriqué en titane et imprimé en 3D en une seule pièce, s'est déployé comme un « jack-in-the-box » en orbite autour de la Terre. Une caméra à bord a filmé le déploiement fluide du ressort au-dessus de l'océan Pacifique.

**Impact** – Le ressort JACC est unique car il combine plusieurs pièces en une seule, ce qui le rend plus léger, plus simple et moins coûteux à produire. Il utilise trois fois moins de pièces que des ressorts traditionnels similaires et a la taille d’une petite boîte. Ce test réussi montre que l'impression 3D peut aider à fabriquer de meilleures antennes spatiales qui se replient pour le lancement et s'ouvrent précisément dans l'espace.

**Prochaines étapes** – Le JPL continuera à développer et tester ces pièces imprimées en 3D ainsi que d'autres nouveaux modèles d'antennes lors de futures missions. Ces technologies pourraient rendre les satellites et instruments spatiaux plus petits, moins chers et plus fiables.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le JPL de la NASA a démontré qu’une pièce imprimée en 3D peut simplifier et améliorer le matériel spatial en déployant avec succès un ressort léger en orbite.
articles.personality_title_es: El resorte impreso en 3D de la NASA se despliega con éxito en el espacio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Laboratorio de Propulsión a Chorro (JPL) de la NASA está explorando nuevas formas de fabricar piezas para naves espaciales usando impresión 3D, también llamada fabricación aditiva. Este método puede crear piezas complejas más rápido y barato que los métodos tradicionales.

**Qué pasó** – El 3 de febrero de 2026, una pequeña nave llamada Mercury One, lanzada desde California en noviembre de 2025, probó un resorte especial llamado JPL Additive Compliant Canister (JACC). Este resorte, hecho de titanio e impreso en 3D como una sola pieza, saltó como un «muñeco de caja» mientras orbitaba la Tierra. Una cámara a bordo grabó el despliegue suave del resorte sobre el Océano Pacífico.

**Impacto** – El resorte JACC es único porque combina varias piezas en una sola, haciéndolo más ligero, simple y barato de producir. Usa tres veces menos piezas que resortes similares tradicionales y tiene el tamaño de una caja pequeña. Esta prueba exitosa muestra que la impresión 3D puede ayudar a construir mejores antenas espaciales que se pliegan para el lanzamiento y se abren con precisión en el espacio.

**Próximo paso** – JPL seguirá desarrollando y probando estas piezas impresas en 3D junto con otros nuevos diseños de antenas en futuras misiones. Estas tecnologías podrían ayudar a hacer satélites e instrumentos espaciales más pequeños, baratos y confiables.

**Frase clave** – El JPL de la NASA demostró que las piezas impresas en 3D pueden simplificar y mejorar el equipo espacial al desplegar con éxito un resorte liviano en órbita.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a small, intricately 3D-printed titanium spring unfolding like a jack-in-the-box against the backdrop of a serene Earth horizon viewed from low orbit, with soft natural blues and greens of the ocean and Antarctica below, symbolizing advanced space technology and innovation through a single compact mechanism.
